name: Walgreens Hub Architecture – 50 Card Deck
description: Flashcards covering the technical and logical architecture of Walgreens Hub Solutions, including Scriptly, Twilio, TransferMyRx, portals, AWS hosting, SSO, and data flows.

cards.question: What is the primary architectural role of Scriptly within Walgreens Hub Solutions?

a.text: It serves as the core non-dispensing pharmacy management platform that orchestrates Hub workflows, tasks, and patient-support activities.
a.explanation: Scriptly is the Hub’s central workflow engine, not a dispensing system.

b.text: It is a point-of-sale system used in all retail stores for payment processing.
b.explanation: Retail POS is separate from Hub architecture.

c.text: It is a dedicated inventory management system for specialty pharmacies only.
c.explanation: Inventory is not Scriptly’s primary role.

d.text: It is a cloud data warehouse used exclusively for analytics.
d.explanation: Analytics use Hub data but Scriptly itself is transactional.

cards.explanation: Scriptly underpins Hub operations as a non-dispensing, workflow-focused pharmacy management platform that coordinates enrollment, PA, BI, outreach, and reporting.

cards.question: Which frontend technology stack does Scriptly primarily use?

a.text: Vue with TypeScript, hosted behind CDN/edge services.
a.explanation: Scriptly uses a modern SPA built with Vue and TypeScript.

b.text: Classic ASP with server-side VBScript pages.
b.explanation: This reflects legacy tech, not Scriptly.

c.text: AngularJS 1.x hosted on on-prem IIS.
c.explanation: AngularJS 1.x is not the chosen stack.

d.text: Native Windows desktop client built with WPF.
d.explanation: Scriptly is browser-based, not a desktop app.

cards.explanation: A Vue + TypeScript SPA enables rapid updates and a responsive UX while being served through modern cloud hosting and CDN layers.

cards.question: Which backend technology powers Scriptly’s primary API layer?

a.text: .NET (modern .NET) web APIs running on Linux-based application servers.
a.explanation: Scriptly’s backend is implemented with modern .NET services.

b.text: PHP monolith hosted on a shared cPanel environment.
b.explanation: This does not match the Hub architecture.

c.text: Node.js serverless functions only.
c.explanation: Hub APIs are not purely serverless Node.js.

d.text: Mainframe COBOL applications exposed via 3270 emulation.
d.explanation: Legacy mainframes are not Scriptly’s core API layer.

cards.explanation: Modern .NET APIs provide robust, performant services for Scriptly’s application logic and integration points.

cards.question: What type of database platform underlies Scriptly’s core transactional storage?

a.text: Relational SQL database (Microsoft SQL Server) hosted in the cloud.
a.explanation: Scriptly uses a managed relational database for transactional integrity.

b.text: A local SQLite database on each agent workstation.
b.explanation: Local DBs would not support centralized workflows.

c.text: A schemaless document database running entirely in the browser.
c.explanation: This does not align with enterprise requirements.

d.text: A tape-based archival system accessed via batch jobs.
d.explanation: Tape is used for archival, not transactional operations.

cards.explanation: Relational SQL storage supports strong consistency, structured workflows, and complex reporting for Hub operations.

cards.question: How is Scriptly primarily exposed to Hub users inside the contact center?

a.text: Scriptly is embedded (iframed) inside the Twilio Flex agent desktop.
a.explanation: Agents interact with Scriptly directly within Flex to avoid context switching.

b.text: Agents SSH into Scriptly servers and run CLI tools.
b.explanation: This would be unusable for call center workflows.

c.text: Agents remote into a Citrix desktop that runs a local Scriptly EXE.
c.explanation: Scriptly is web-based, not a remote desktop app.

d.text: Scriptly is accessed only via mobile apps on agents’ phones.
d.explanation: Agents primarily use desktop web UIs.

cards.explanation: Embedding Scriptly into Flex consolidates telephony and workflow tools into a unified agent experience.

cards.question: What is the primary role of Twilio Flex in Hub architecture?

a.text: It acts as the cloud contact center platform handling IVR, call routing, and agent softphone functionality.
a.explanation: Flex is the contact center layer integrated with Scriptly.

b.text: It serves as the core prescription dispensing system.
b.explanation: Dispensing occurs in pharmacy systems, not Flex.

c.text: It is the main EMR used by prescribers.
c.explanation: Prescribers use their own EHR/EMR systems.

d.text: It provides SSO authentication for all Walgreens applications.
d.explanation: SSO is handled via Walgreens identity systems, not Flex.

cards.explanation: Twilio Flex manages inbound/outbound calling, queues, and skilling, while Scriptly manages clinical and operational workflows.

cards.question: Which component in Twilio is used to build the Hub’s IVR and call flows?

a.text: Twilio Studio and programmable voice flows.
a.explanation: Studio is used to design IVR call flows and logic.

b.text: Twilio SendGrid email workflows.
b.explanation: SendGrid is for email, not IVR.

c.text: Twilio Conversations for SMS chatbots only.
c.explanation: Conversations focuses on messaging, not IVR routing.

d.text: Twilio Frontline mobile app configurations.
d.explanation: Frontline is not the Hub’s IVR engine.

cards.explanation: Twilio Studio orchestrates IVR branching, prompts, and integration with Scriptly APIs.

cards.question: What is the purpose of Twilio Voice Insights in the Hub stack?

a.text: To provide diagnostics and metrics on call quality, latency, and failures.
a.explanation: Voice Insights helps troubleshoot and monitor voice performance.

b.text: To encrypt prescription data at rest.
b.explanation: Encryption is handled at the infrastructure and DB layer.

c.text: To manage user access roles in Scriptly.
c.explanation: Access is managed in Scriptly and identity systems.

d.text: To store audio files in the core transactional database.
d.explanation: Audio storage and monitoring are separate concerns.

cards.explanation: Voice Insights allows operations and vendor teams to diagnose call issues impacting agent experiences.

cards.question: How are inbound phone calls generally routed into the Hub architecture?

a.text: Calls hit a Walgreens-owned number, forward into a Twilio-managed number, traverse Twilio infrastructure, then terminate at the agent web client.
a.explanation: This describes the typical call routing path.

b.text: Calls terminate directly on Scriptly servers via SIP trunks.
b.explanation: Scriptly is not a telephony endpoint.

c.text: Calls are translated into emails and delivered via SMTP.
c.explanation: Email is not the primary inbound channel for calls.

d.text: Calls go through a mainframe PBX and are mirrored to Twilio only for logging.
d.explanation: Twilio actively handles routing, not just logging.

cards.explanation: Walgreens numbers front-end the experience while Twilio manages routing and connection to agent desktops.

cards.question: What is the primary architectural role of TransferMyRx in the Hub ecosystem?

a.text: It provides secure, structured prescription transfer capabilities between Scriptly, ScriptMed, and external pharmacies.
a.explanation: TMRx facilitates electronic transfers beyond manual phone/fax workflows.

b.text: It is an analytics-only system used for BI dashboards.
b.explanation: Analytics are fed by multiple sources, not solely TMRx.

c.text: It manages inventory levels within community pharmacies.
c.explanation: Inventory management is separate from TMRx.

d.text: It replaces SureScripts for all e-prescribing.
d.explanation: SureScripts remains a core prescribing network.

cards.explanation: TransferMyRx provides a structured way to transfer Rx records electronically between different pharmacy systems as part of Hub workflows.

cards.question: When Scriptly cannot transfer a prescription via e-prescribing channels, what is the typical fallback mechanism?

a.text: Manual or electronic fax to the target pharmacy.
a.explanation: Fax remains a key fallback when electronic transfers fail.

b.text: SMS text message with prescription details to the patient.
b.explanation: Prescription details are not sent via SMS for legal and privacy reasons.

c.text: Posting the prescription to a public portal without authentication.
c.explanation: This would be a major security violation.

d.text: Uploading the prescription to social media for the pharmacy to see.
d.explanation: Obviously not permissible.

cards.explanation: When eRx and TMRx routes fail, fax workflows backstop prescription transfers within compliance constraints.

cards.question: Which core architectural pattern describes how Hub interacts with Walgreens’ broader pharmacy network?

a.text: Receive, Rescue, Redirect model layered on top of existing Walgreens retail and specialty systems.
a.explanation: The Hub sits between prescriber/patient flows and downstream dispensing.

b.text: Hub directly replaces all existing dispensing systems with a single monolithic platform.
b.explanation: Hub augments, not replaces, core dispensing platforms.

c.text: Hub runs entirely offline with no integration to other systems.
c.explanation: Online integrations are essential.

d.text: Hub controls retail store POS, inventory, and staffing directly.
d.explanation: These remain within existing Walgreens platforms.

cards.explanation: The Hub intercepts eligible prescriptions, applies support workflows, then returns them to dispensing locations.

cards.question: What is the function of the patient, provider, manufacturer, and FRM portals in the Hub architecture?

a.text: They provide role-specific, external-facing access to Scriptly-managed data and workflows.
a.explanation: Each portal is tailored to a stakeholder’s view and needs.

b.text: They are simple static websites with marketing content only.
b.explanation: Portals are interactive and data-driven.

c.text: They host Walgreens’ public e-commerce store.
c.explanation: E-commerce is handled by other systems.

d.text: They replace all EMR systems used by prescribers.
d.explanation: Portals complement but do not replace EMRs.

cards.explanation: Portals expose curated views into Hub data to support coordination across stakeholders.

cards.question: Which identity mechanism is used for internal Walgreens users accessing Scriptly?

a.text: Walgreens OneID Single Sign-On integrated via SSO protocols.
a.explanation: Hub users authenticate through Walgreens’ enterprise identity provider.

b.text: Local Scriptly username and password accounts only.
b.explanation: Local accounts would create security and lifecycle issues.

c.text: Hard-coded credentials stored on agent desktops.
c.explanation: This would be highly insecure.

d.text: Anonymous access with IP allowlisting only.
d.explanation: Hub access is not anonymous.

cards.explanation: OneID SSO centralizes access control, provisioning, and revocation for internal users.

cards.question: What happens architecturally when a user’s OneID access is revoked?

a.text: Scriptly access is automatically disabled while keeping the user record for audit purposes.
a.explanation: Identity is upstream; Scriptly reflects that change.

b.text: Scriptly silently falls back to a shared generic account.
b.explanation: Shared accounts would break audit requirements.

c.text: Scriptly migrates the user to a local backup account with full rights.
c.explanation: This would defeat access control.

d.text: Nothing changes in Scriptly; the user can still log in indefinitely.
d.explanation: This would violate security policies.

cards.explanation: Tight coupling between OneID and Scriptly ensures timely revocation of access while preserving audit history.

cards.question: How is data in transit typically protected within Hub architecture?

a.text: By enforcing HTTPS/TLS encryption on web and API traffic.
a.explanation: TLS is standard for securing data in transit.

b.text: By sending all traffic over unencrypted HTTP inside a VPN only.
b.explanation: VPN alone is not sufficient for PHI-grade security.

c.text: By storing all data on local desktops instead of transmitting it.
c.explanation: Hub requires communication; local-only is not realistic.

d.text: By emailing raw PHI between systems on internal email servers.
d.explanation: Email is not a primary integration channel for PHI.

cards.explanation: TLS across web and API endpoints is required to protect PHI and sensitive data in transit.

cards.question: How is data at rest typically protected for Scriptly’s core database and file storage?

a.text: Using cloud-native encryption-at-rest mechanisms (e.g., DB and storage volume encryption).
a.explanation: Managed services encrypt data at rest with platform keys or customer-managed keys.

b.text: Relying only on obscurity of database table names.
b.explanation: Security by obscurity is not sufficient.

c.text: Storing unencrypted PHI on developer laptops.
c.explanation: Local unencrypted PHI is prohibited.

d.text: Writing all PHI to plain-text log files on each agent machine.
d.explanation: Plain-text PHI on endpoints is unacceptable.

cards.explanation: Encryption-at-rest is a fundamental requirement for PHI in the cloud.

cards.question: What is the role of audit logging in Scriptly’s architecture?

a.text: To capture all changes to key entities and events for compliance, traceability, and investigation.
a.explanation: Detailed audit logs support regulatory and operational needs.

b.text: To store marketing metrics like website clicks and ad impressions.
b.explanation: Scriptly logs focus on operational events, not marketing analytics.

c.text: To replace database backups.
c.explanation: Audit logs and backups serve different purposes.

d.text: To randomly delete old patient records for storage savings.
d.explanation: Record retention follows defined policies, not random deletion.

cards.explanation: Audit logging is essential for regulated healthcare workflows and internal controls.

cards.question: Which browsers are officially supported for accessing Scriptly at the Hub?

a.text: Microsoft Edge and Google Chrome.
a.explanation: Walgreens standardizes on Edge but also supports Chrome.

b.text: Only Internet Explorer 8.
b.explanation: IE8 is deprecated and unsupported.

c.text: Only Safari on iOS.
c.explanation: Mobile browsers are not the primary agent platform.

d.text: Only Firefox ESR.
d.explanation: Firefox is not the documented standard.

cards.explanation: Edge is the corporate standard; Chrome is supported for compatibility.

cards.question: Where is Scriptly’s frontend typically hosted in the cloud architecture?

a.text: On object storage (e.g., S3) fronted by a CDN or edge network.
a.explanation: Static SPA assets are ideal for CDN-backed hosting.

b.text: On each agent workstation as a local file share.
b.explanation: Local hosting would be impossible to maintain.

c.text: Directly from a relational database via dynamic HTML generation.
c.explanation: This is not how SPAs are delivered.

d.text: On a tape drive accessed by a web gateway.
d.explanation: Tape is unsuitable for serving live web assets.

cards.explanation: Hosting SPAs on object storage behind a CDN improves performance and resilience.

cards.question: What component shields Scriptly APIs from direct exposure to the public internet while providing performance benefits?

a.text: An edge or reverse-proxy layer (e.g., Cloudflare or similar).
a.explanation: Reverse proxies handle TLS, caching, and security policies at the edge.

b.text: Direct RDP access from external IPs.
b.explanation: RDP should never front APIs to the public.

c.text: A consumer home router at the data center.
c.explanation: Consumer routers are not enterprise edge devices.

d.text: Plain HTTP listeners exposed on port 80 only.
d.explanation: Unprotected HTTP is not acceptable.

cards.explanation: Reverse-proxy/CDN layers protect, accelerate, and observe API traffic.

cards.question: How does the Hub ensure that Twilio support is managed without Walgreens directly holding a Twilio SLA?

a.text: Scriptly acts as the sole escalation and support interface for Twilio-related issues.
a.explanation: Walgreens routes Twilio problems through Scriptly’s support model.

b.text: Walgreens disables Twilio monitoring entirely.
b.explanation: Monitoring is crucial and not disabled.

c.text: Twilio provides direct access to its internal engineering chat to all Hub agents.
c.explanation: This is not how vendor relationships operate.

d.text: Hub agents open tickets directly in Twilio’s developer forums.
d.explanation: Support flows through the Scriptly vendor relationship.

cards.explanation: Scriptly owns the Twilio relationship so Hub teams can work with a single vendor for both app and telephony issues.

cards.question: What is the architectural relationship between PROMOTE and the Hub?

a.text: PROMOTE supports community store workflows and acts as a touchpoint for routing referrals into or from the Hub.
a.explanation: PROMOTE is part of the broader routing ecosystem.

b.text: PROMOTE is a replacement for Scriptly inside the Hub.
b.explanation: Scriptly is the Hub platform; PROMOTE lives in the community store side.

c.text: PROMOTE is the primary contact center telephony platform.
c.explanation: Telephony is handled by Twilio Flex.

d.text: PROMOTE is a cloud data warehouse used for analytics only.
d.explanation: PROMOTE is more than just analytics.

cards.explanation: PROMOTE helps community pharmacies interact with Hub-eligible drugs and referrals.

cards.question: How are community store prescriptions identified as Hub-eligible in the architecture?

a.text: By configuration and manual selection in systems like PROMOTE that flag referrals to the Hub.
a.explanation: Config-driven plus human workflows determine Hub routing.

b.text: By a hard-coded list in each store’s local spreadsheet only.
b.explanation: Pure spreadsheets are not the authoritative mechanism.

c.text: By scanning QR codes on prescription bottles generated by the Hub.
c.explanation: This is not the described workflow.

d.text: By social media posts from patients indicating they want Hub support.
d.explanation: Social media is not part of eligibility detection.

cards.explanation: Config and manual workflows inside community systems route Hub-eligible Rxs into Hub processes.

cards.question: How does the Hub architecture support manufacturers’ need for real-time dashboards and reporting?

a.text: By aggregating Scriptly workflow data and exposing it through manufacturer and FRM portals.
a.explanation: Portals provide near real-time insights into patient journeys.

b.text: By emailing weekly static spreadsheets only.
b.explanation: Static spreadsheets alone would not be considered modern real-time reporting.

c.text: By providing direct read-write access to the transactional database.
c.explanation: External parties should not have direct DB access.

d.text: By posting raw PHI publicly and letting manufacturers scrape it.
d.explanation: This would be completely non-compliant.

cards.explanation: Manufacturers get curated, compliant visibility through role-specific portals rather than direct DB access.

cards.question: Which component of the architecture provides call recording and workforce management capabilities?

a.text: A workforce management and recording platform (e.g., Verint) integrated with the contact center stack.
a.explanation: Verint-type tools cover call recording and WFM.

b.text: The core Scriptly transactional database.
b.explanation: Scriptly stores workflow data, not WFM and audio.

c.text: The manufacturer portal.
c.explanation: Portals are for external stakeholders, not WFM.

d.text: The retail POS system.
d.explanation: POS systems do not perform call recording.

cards.explanation: Call recording and workforce management are typically handled by specialized platforms integrated with Twilio Flex.

cards.question: Why is Scriptly described as a non-dispensing pharmacy system in the Hub architecture?

a.text: Because it focuses on patient support workflows, enrollment, PA, and triage, while final dispensing occurs in separate pharmacy systems.
a.explanation: Hub separates support from actual dispensing.

b.text: Because it cannot see any prescription information at all.
b.explanation: Scriptly absolutely manages script workflows.

c.text: Because it only handles cash payments.
c.explanation: Financial flows are not limited to cash.

d.text: Because it runs offline without any network.
d.explanation: It is a cloud and network-dependent system.

cards.explanation: This separation allows Hub to operate as a centralized support engine without replacing existing dispensing platforms.

cards.question: How does the Hub architecture support multiple points of patient entry across the Walgreens network?

a.text: By integrating with retail and specialty pharmacies, call center channels, and digital portals, all feeding eligible Rxs into Scriptly workflows.
a.explanation: Hub centralizes support from multiple entry points.

b.text: By requiring all patients to call a single 1-800 number only.
b.explanation: There are multiple entry channels.

c.text: By forcing all Rxs to go through a single specialty pharmacy.
c.explanation: Hub supports multiple dispensing endpoints.

d.text: By disabling e-prescribing so that only paper scripts are accepted.
d.explanation: E-prescribing remains a key channel.

cards.explanation: A multi-entry architecture allows Hub to capture more patients who might otherwise bypass support programs.

cards.question: Which of the following best describes the Hub’s segregation of environments?

a.text: Separate environments (e.g., non-prod and prod) with controlled access to protect PHI and maintain change management discipline.
a.explanation: Segregated environments are critical in regulated healthcare systems.

b.text: A single production environment used for development, testing, and training.
b.explanation: This would violate best practices and risk data integrity.

c.text: Developer laptops acting as the primary production servers.
c.explanation: Production workloads do not run on laptops.

d.text: A test environment exposed publicly without authentication for convenience.
d.explanation: Unauthenticated test environments with PHI would be unacceptable.

cards.explanation: Proper environment segregation minimizes risk and aligns with enterprise change management and PHI protection requirements.

cards.question: How is skilling and queue assignment handled for calls in the Hub?

a.text: Within Twilio Flex, which assigns calls to agent queues based on defined skills and attributes.
a.explanation: Flex manages skilling and queue logic for voice.

b.text: By manually emailing a list of calls to agents.
b.explanation: Email is not a real-time routing mechanism.

c.text: By selecting random agents each time a call arrives.
c.explanation: Random assignment would break SLAs.

d.text: By letting patients choose any agent directly in the portal.
d.explanation: Patients do not pick individual agents.

cards.explanation: Skilling in Flex ensures calls reach agents with appropriate capabilities for specific workflows.

cards.question: What integration point allows IVR flows to interact with Scriptly workflows?

a.text: APIs exposed by Scriptly that Twilio Studio or backend services can call to create or update records.
a.explanation: API integration enables IVR-driven workflow changes.

b.text: Direct access from Twilio Studio to Scriptly’s SQL tables.
b.explanation: Studio does not directly touch DBs.

c.text: File drops on agent desktops processed manually.
c.explanation: Manual file drops would not be real-time.

d.text: Screen scraping of Scriptly pages by Twilio.
d.explanation: Screen scraping is brittle and not the integration pattern.

cards.explanation: Scriptly APIs allow IVR actions (e.g., capturing DTMF responses) to be translated into workflow updates.

cards.question: Why does the Hub rely on structured tasks and queues rather than ad-hoc email requests to manage work?

a.text: Tasks and queues provide traceability, SLA management, routing, and reporting for operational work.
a.explanation: Architecture must support observability and control.

b.text: Email is prohibited by all regulators in healthcare.
b.explanation: Email is allowed but insufficient as the core work engine.

c.text: Agents do not have email accounts.
c.explanation: Agents may have email but work is managed via systems.

d.text: Queues exist only for historical reasons and are never used.
d.explanation: Queues are actively used to run operations.

cards.explanation: An architecture based on tasks and queues ensures reliable, auditable operational workflows.

cards.question: How does the Hub architecture support HIPAA-compliant consent capture?

a.text: By recording consent events and documents within Scriptly workflows and storing them in secure, auditable systems.
a.explanation: Consent must be recorded and auditable.

b.text: By assuming consent for all patients once they fill any prescription at Walgreens.
b.explanation: Consent cannot be presumed.

c.text: By verbally confirming consent without recording it anywhere.
c.explanation: Unrecorded verbal consent is not sufficient for audit.

d.text: By placing a short notice on social media and treating all likes as consent.
d.explanation: Social media interactions are not consent.

cards.explanation: Consent capture is integrated into workflows and storage so it can be verified for compliance and reporting.

cards.question: Which statement best describes the boundary between Hub architecture and Walgreens’ core retail systems?

a.text: The Hub operates as an overlay and integration layer, connecting to but not replacing retail dispensing, POS, and EMR systems.
a.explanation: Hub extends capabilities rather than rewriting the enterprise core.

b.text: The Hub fully replaces all retail systems, which are decommissioned.
b.explanation: Retail systems remain essential.

c.text: The Hub has no connection at all to retail systems and operates in isolation.
c.explanation: Hub must integrate to be effective.

d.text: The Hub takes direct control of store staffing, scheduling, and payroll.
d.explanation: These functions stay outside Hub scope.

cards.explanation: Hub architecture is designed to augment core systems and leverage Walgreens’ nationwide footprint while adding high-touch support workflows.
